title: Spoofed duplicate contacts bypass key-update warning
description: The commit introduces `addedWithCandidate` for protected contact imports, but the import workflow treats that result exactly like a normal add. When an imported certificate has the same email or user ID as an existing contact but a different fingerprint, `performProtectedAddContact` creates a new identity, marks the key preferred if it is encryptable, persists it, and returns `addedWithCandidate`. `ContactImportWorkflow` discards the `ContactCandidateMatch` and calls success without showing the user that the new key appears to belong to an existing contact. The spoofed duplicate identity then participates in recipient resolution through `publicKeysForRecipientContactIDs`, so future encryption can target the attacker-controlled preferred key if the user selects the duplicate contact with the expected display name. This regresses the prior key-update warning/confirmation behavior for conflicting contact identities and creates a contact-impersonation/confidentiality risk through crafted QR/custom-URL/file public-key imports.

title: GH_TOKEN exposed to entire XCFramework build
description: The new GH_TOKEN environment variable is scoped to the whole build-xcframework.sh step rather than only to the gh release list/download commands that need it. The build script then invokes cargo build and cargo run multiple times using env without removing inherited variables, so workspace code, build.rs scripts, proc macros, and dependency build scripts can read GH_TOKEN. In PR checks this exposes a read-only repository token to pull_request code; in the stable and edge release workflows the same pattern exposes a contents:write token during release builds. A malicious dependency build script or malicious code executed in an approved release build could exfiltrate the token and use it during its lifetime to access or modify repository/release contents. The token should be passed only to the gh download calls, or explicitly unset for all Cargo/build subprocesses.

title: Release workflow runs arbitrary refs with write token
description: The workflow_dispatch path accepts a free-form source_ref and checks it out before building. The job grants contents:write, id-token:write, and attestations:write globally. actions/checkout is used without persist-credentials: false, so the write-scoped GITHUB_TOKEN is persisted in the checkout for subsequent commands. The workflow then executes Cargo tests, the build script, xcodebuild, and Python scripts from the selected ref. A malicious ref can therefore run arbitrary code in a release job with write credentials, mint OIDC tokens, push tags/releases, or tamper with produced artifacts. For create_release=true, the workflow only validates the release tag's string format and project version, then creates/uploads assets using COMMIT_SHA from the checked-out source_ref. It does not verify that the checked-out commit is the commit pointed to by the stable release tag. This can allow malicious or accidental publication of stable release assets built from a different commit than the verified tag.

title: Rust audit does not gate release publication
description: The commit adds `rust-dependency-audit` jobs to the release workflows, but the release-producing jobs do not declare `needs: rust-dependency-audit`. As a result, `publish-stable-release` and `publish-edge-release` can build, attest, tag, and publish assets even when `cargo audit --deny warnings` finds a vulnerable or yanked Rust dependency. The documentation explicitly notes that packaging and release jobs still run without depending on the audit. For an immutable stable release, this means known-vulnerable Rust code can be published before or despite the audit failure, leaving downstream users or release consumers with compromised artifacts unless a human notices and remediates after the fact. The release jobs should depend on the audit job, or the audit should run as an early step inside the release job before any asset creation, attestation, tag creation, or release upload.

title: Untrusted certifications can mark contacts certified
description: The new persistence flow treats `verification.status == .valid` as sufficient to create and save a certification artifact. However, the verification candidate set is `availableContacts + ownKeys`, which includes unverified contacts and can include the target certificate itself. A malicious contact can provide a certification signature from an untrusted key, or potentially an extracted self-certification from the target certificate, and the app can save it as a valid artifact. The projection logic then marks the target key as `certified` if any artifact has `validationStatus == .valid`, and the contact detail UI displays this as a green OpenPGP Certification state. This does not directly change manual fingerprint verification or recipient selection, but it creates persistent misleading trust metadata that can influence user decisions.

title: TOCTOU can delete active protected-data root secret
description: The commit introduces ProtectedDataFirstDomainSharedRightCleaner and wires it into first protected-domain bootstrap/migration paths. The cleaner decides whether a shared right is orphaned from a caller-supplied registry snapshot and then checks only for non-registry artifacts before deleting the persisted shared right. It does not re-load the registry immediately before deletion and does not run under the registry mutation gate. A concurrent first-domain creation can persist the root secret before staging artifacts. During that window, another caller that loaded an earlier empty registry can see the newly persisted root secret, see no artifacts yet, and delete it as if it were orphaned. The concurrent transaction can then finish and commit domain membership with artifacts encrypted under a root secret that no longer exists, making protected settings/private-key-control data unrecoverable until reset. The cleanup should be serialized with registry mutations or should re-check the current registry state immediately before deletion and abort if any pending mutation or committed membership is present.

title: Local reset fails open when authentication is unavailable
description: SettingsScreenModel.confirmLocalDataReset gates authentication with authManager.canEvaluate(appSessionPolicy:). When canEvaluate returns false, for example because biometrics-only app protection is unavailable/locked out or device-owner authentication is unavailable, the if block is skipped and localDataResetService.resetAllLocalData() is still called. The reset service then deletes CypherAir keychain items, the protected-data root secret, protected storage, contacts, defaults, and temporary artifacts. This is a fail-open authentication decision for a destructive security-sensitive operation; an attacker with local access to the app/settings UI in an authentication-unavailable state can wipe private keys and protected app data without satisfying the configured authentication policy.

title: Stale operation prompt generation can disable privacy blur
description: The commit increments a global operation authentication generation when an operation prompt begins, but the privacy lifecycle gate only observes that generation from later lifecycle callbacks. When the gate first sees a newer generation, it unconditionally arms suppression. If an operation authentication prompt completes without producing a lifecycle notification that the gate observes, or if a genuine resign/inactive notification is the first later callback, the real focus-loss event is suppressed. On macOS, the didResignActive handler then returns before calling handleSceneDidResignActive(), so the privacy screen is not blurred; the following didBecomeActive can also be consumed without running handleResume(). This can leave sensitive decrypted/signing/contact UI visible and avoid the expected relock/reauthentication behavior after the user switches away from the app.

title: Post-auth warm-up can clear background privacy blur
description: The new lifecycle path correctly sets the privacy screen when iOS enters `.background`, even during an authentication prompt. However, `handleResume` now awaits `runPostAuthenticationWarmUpIfNeeded()` after a successful privacy authentication and then always sets `isPrivacyScreenBlurred = false`. The warm-up is wired to `ProtectedSettingsHost.warmUpAfterAppUnlock()`, which can open protected settings and may invoke protected-data authorization. If the user backgrounds the app while this warm-up is pending or being cancelled, `handleSceneDidEnterBackground()` sets the blur to true, but the still-running `handleResume` task later resumes, swallows any warm-up error, and clears the blur without checking whether the app is still foreground-active. This creates a race where sensitive content can be visible in the multitasking switcher/background snapshot. The fix should track current foreground/background state or a scene generation and only clear the blur when the app is active; alternatively cancel/skip warm-up on background and keep the privacy screen set until a foreground resume completes.

title: Unescaped source ref in release verification command
description: The workflow now permits non-main manual drill releases and records RELEASE_SOURCE_REF directly from GITHUB_REF. That ref is later embedded verbatim inside a double-quoted shell command in release-notes.md: `--source-ref "$RELEASE_SOURCE_REF"`. Git ref names can contain shell metacharacters such as double quotes, backticks, dollar signs, and command-substitution syntax. For example, a drill branch named with `$(...)` or an embedded quote/semicolon sequence can cause the rendered verification command in the official GitHub release notes to execute attacker-controlled commands when a developer copies the documented verification block. The workflow validates release_tag_prefix, but it does not validate or shell-escape RELEASE_SOURCE_REF before placing it in a shell command or JSON metadata. The safer pattern is to either restrict accepted drill refs to a conservative character set or render the command using robust shell quoting, e.g. generate escaped arguments with `printf '%q'`, and generate JSON with `jq` rather than raw heredocs.

title: Production auth can be bypassed via UI-test defaults key
description: AuthenticationManager.evaluate now returns success whenever the UserDefaults key com.cypherair.preference.uiTestBypassAuthentication is true. Although AppContainer.makeUITest writes this key into an isolated UI-test defaults suite, the production AuthenticationManager is constructed with UserDefaults.standard and performs the same bypass check. On macOS, a local process running as the user can set the app preference and cause app-session authentication, including the privacy screen unlock path, to succeed without a LocalAuthentication prompt. This does not appear to bypass Secure Enclave access controls for private-key unwraps, but it does bypass the app-level lock protecting visible app state and can keep the session marked authenticated.

title: Import confirmation can act on a replaced key request
description: This commit replaces per-view pending import state with a mutable ImportConfirmationCoordinator. The coordinator's present() method blindly overwrites any existing request. The sheet displays keyInfo/profile from the request passed to the sheet content closure, but the Verify/Add and Add Unverified buttons call coordinator.confirmVerified() / confirmUnverified(), which dereference coordinator.request at button-tap time. If a second import request arrives while a confirmation sheet is already open, for example via the cypherair:// URL handler or another in-app import path using the shared environment coordinator, the visible confirmation can be decoupled from the action that is executed. A user may verify the fingerprint shown for one key while the action imports and marks a different, attacker-supplied key as verified or unverified. This matters because contact public-key integrity controls future encryption and trust decisions.

title: Secret key zeroization skipped on KMS helper failures
description: The commit refactors key generation and import so the Rust engine work happens inside @concurrent helper methods. In the old code, a defer to resetBytes was installed immediately after engine.generateKey or engine.importSecretKey returned, before any later parsing/profile/armoring operations could fail. In the new code, that defer exists only in the public method after the helper returns successfully. If generateKeyOffMainActor obtains a GeneratedKey and parseKeyInfo then throws, or if importKeyOffMainActor obtains unencrypted secretKeyData and parseKeyInfo/detectProfile/armorPublicKey then throws, the helper exits through catch without wiping the secret-key Data it already produced. This leaves raw OpenPGP secret key material to be freed normally rather than explicitly zeroized, increasing exposure to crash dumps, memory forensics, or a later memory disclosure on the device. The import path is especially relevant because it processes user-supplied secret-key files.

title: Decrypted file can persist after cancelled/abandoned decrypt
description: The commit changed file decryption from in-memory plaintext to a streaming service that writes plaintext to a temporary file and returns its URL. DecryptView only deletes decryptedFileURL during onDisappear/contentClear, but it does not cancel an in-flight decrypt task in those handlers. If the user starts a large file decrypt and leaves the view or the app clears content while the operation is still running, decryptedFileURL is still nil so cleanup removes nothing; the background task can continue, write the plaintext file under tmp/decrypted, and assign the URL after the cleanup point has already passed. A similar residual-file path exists when the user cancels after the Rust streaming call has successfully written the output but before Swift stores decryptedFileURL: Task.checkCancellation throws and the CancellationError handler does not remove result.outputURL. This bypasses the intended PRD cleanup for decrypted files and can leave decrypted plaintext in the app container until a later app launch cleanup or manual removal.

title: Signing key not zeroized on no-default encrypt-to-self error
description: In EncryptionService.encrypt, the signing private key is unwrapped first when signWithFingerprint is provided. The new encrypt-to-self default-key guard can then throw CypherAirError.noKeySelected if keyManagement.defaultKey is nil. Because the defer that resets signingKey is declared after this guard, that newly introduced error path exits without calling resetBytes on the unwrapped private key Data. Swift will eventually release the Data object, but the code no longer performs the explicit best-effort zeroization used on all other paths, leaving private key material more likely to remain in process heap memory until reused. This is reachable for API callers, tests, or inconsistent app metadata states where a signing key can be selected while no key is marked default. The fix is to either validate the default key before unwrapping the signing key or install the zeroization defer immediately after declaring signingKey and before any later throwing operation.

title: Decrypt view can show stale signature for wrong content
description: The refactor merges text and file decryption into one view but keeps a single shared signatureVerification value. Text results and file results are mode-specific and retained independently, while the signature section is rendered outside the mode-specific result blocks. Both decryptText() and decryptFile() overwrite the same signatureVerification. As a result, a user can decrypt an unsigned text message, then decrypt a signed file, then switch back to text mode and see the old text plaintext displayed together with the file's valid signature status. This can mislead the user into trusting unauthenticated or differently authenticated plaintext. The same problem can occur in the opposite direction for file output. The state should be bound atomically to the decrypted content/mode, or separate text/file signature states should be used and cleared on mode changes, input edits, and file selection.

title: Oversized FFI inputs can crash Swift callers
description: The commit adds Swift bindings that expose PGP operations such as decrypt, encrypt, and parseS2kParams. These methods accept Swift Data that may originate from attacker-controlled OpenPGP files or imported keys. During lowering, FfiConverterData.write converts value.count with Int32(value.count), and ForeignBytes does the same for the serialized RustBuffer. Swift's Int32 initializer traps on values larger than Int32.max rather than throwing. Because these traps occur before or inside try!/FFI marshalling, they are not converted into PgpError. A maliciously large file or imported key payload can therefore crash the app process, causing a local denial of service. The binding should enforce explicit maximum input sizes and return a normal error before marshalling, or use a non-trapping checked conversion path.

title: Selector discovery exposes unauthenticated User IDs
description: discover_certificate_selectors parses the certificate with Sequoia, computes state from cert.userids(), but then builds the returned User ID list by iterating RawCert packets and accepting every UserID packet. If a certificate contains an extra bare or invalid User ID packet, it can be returned with displayText and userIdData even when it has no valid self-signature or policy-valid binding. discover_user_id then defaults missing state to primary=false and revoked=false, which makes the injected selector look like a normal non-revoked option. If this catalog is used to drive User ID certification, revocation, or trust-selection UI, a malicious imported certificate can cause the app to present and operate on identities that are not actually authenticated by the certificate owner.

title: Production Xcode target compiles security test mocks
description: The introduced XcodeGen configuration uses `Sources` as the application target source root without excluding `Sources/Security/Mocks`. The generated `.pbxproj` confirms that `MockAuthenticator.swift`, `MockKeychain.swift`, and `MockSecureEnclave.swift` are in the CypherAir app target's Sources build phase. These mocks are explicitly non-production implementations: `MockAuthenticator` defaults to successful authentication, `MockKeychain` stores items in a plain in-memory dictionary while ignoring access-control attributes, and `MockSecureEnclave` uses software key material instead of hardware-bound Secure Enclave keys. The current minimal app does not instantiate them, so this is not a remotely reachable runtime issue in the checked-out skeleton. However, it is a security-relevant build integration flaw: any production key-custody or authentication code compiled in the same module can accidentally or maliciously select these implementations, bypassing the intended LocalAuthentication, Keychain, and Secure Enclave protections.

title: Concurrent tutorial contacts open can fail sandbox setup
description: Previously, the tutorial contacts protected domain was opened synchronously during TutorialSandboxContainer initialization, so the container was returned only after a single open completed. This commit moves the open into TutorialSandboxContainer.openContactsIfNeeded() and calls it from TutorialSessionStore.openModule(). Because openModule is @MainActor async and suspends while opening contacts, a second UI task can re-enter openModule before the first open finishes. The new guard only skips when availability is already .availableProtectedDomain; it does not treat .opening as an in-progress operation or share the first task. A second call can therefore start another detached open against the same ContactService/ContactsDomainStore while the first create/open transaction is pending. If that second open observes a pending registry mutation or otherwise fails, ContactService returns a non-available state, and TutorialSessionStore catches the failure, cleans up the active container, and drops the sandbox. This is a local availability/data-loss bug for the tutorial sandbox rather than a production key/contact confidentiality issue.

title: Text input section is recreated on every edit
description: In DecryptView and VerifyView, the text editor is inside a Section tagged with `.id(textInputSectionEpoch)`. The binding setter for the editor calls an invalidate method whenever the user changes the text. This commit changed those invalidate methods to increment `textInputSectionEpoch`, so every user edit changes the identity of the Section containing the active text editor. In SwiftUI, changing `.id` forces that subtree to be replaced, which can drop keyboard focus, reset selection/cursor/undo state, and make manual text entry unreliable. EncryptView and SignView only increment the epoch after operation completion, but DecryptView and VerifyView also do so from edit-triggered invalidation paths.
